title: Vasculitis — ANCA-associated
oneLiner: Autoimmune small-vessel inflammation (GPA, MPA, EGPA) causing renal, pulmonary, ENT, skin & nerve damage — treatable but fatal if missed.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN) → hyperkalaemia / pulmonary oedema / uraemia.**  
  - Single finding: **oliguria + dipstick haematuria/proteinuria** with rising creatinine >50% in <48 hours.
- **Diffuse alveolar haemorrhage (DAH) → respiratory failure.**  
  - Single finding: **haemoptysis + bilateral airspace opacities on CXR + falling Hb** (not infection — no fever, negative procalcitonin).
- **Myocarditis (especially in EGPA) → cardiogenic shock / arrhythmia.**  
  - Single finding: **troponin rise + ECG changes (ST/T wave) without chest pain** — rule out ischaemia.
sections.Presentation:
- **Constitutional:** fever, weight loss, fatigue, arthralgia, night sweats.
- **Renal:** haematuria (microscopic or visible), oliguria, peripheral oedema, hypertension.
- **Respiratory:** cough, dyspnoea, haemoptysis, pleuritic pain.
- **ENT (GPA):** nasal crusting, epistaxis, sinusitis, saddle-nose deformity, otitis media, subglottic stenosis.
- **Skin:** palpable purpura, ulcers, digital ischaemia, livedo racemosa.
- **Neurological:** mononeuritis multiplex (wrist drop, foot drop), cranial nerve palsy, peripheral neuropathy.
- **Other:** ocular (scleritis, episcleritis), GI (abdominal pain, bleeding), testicular pain.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Infection (endocarditis, TB, fungal)** → positive cultures/high CRP+procalcitonin; no ANCA.
- **Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture’s)** → linear IgG on renal biopsy; anti-GBM positive; ANCA negative.
- **Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)** → ANA+dsDNA; low C3/C4; photosensitive rash, serositis.
- **IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein)** → palpable purpura on lower limbs + IgA dominant immune deposits; ANCA negative.
- **Cryoglobulinaemia** → cryoglobulins + HCV serology; purpura + neuropathy.
- **Lymphoproliferative / other vasculitis** (e.g. PAN, Behçet) — ANCA negative, specific organ patterns.
- **Splitter:** **c-ANCA (PR3)** strongly suggests GPA; **p-ANCA (MPO)** suggests MPA; **p-ANCA+ MPO+ eosinophilia + asthma** → EGPA.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urinalysis (dipstick for blood, protein; microscopy for red cell casts).
  - ECG (qTc screening for cardiac involvement — especially in EGPA).
  - NEWS2 + urine output chart.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, eosinophilia in EGPA), U+E (creatinine rapid rise), LFT, CRP/ESR (very high).
  - ANCA serology: **c-ANCA/PR3** (GPA), **p-ANCA/MPO** (MPA, EGPA). *N.B. ~10% ANCA-negative.*
  - Anti-GBM (to exclude double-positive disease), ANA, complement (C3/C4), cryoglobulins.
  - Blood cultures ×2 (rule out endocarditis).
  - Troponin + BNP if cardiac concern.
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (airspace opacities, cavities, nodules — GPA).
  - CT chest (high-res: nodules, cavitation, ground glass — DAH).
  - CT sinuses (if ENT symptoms — mucosal thickening, bony destruction).
  - Renal ultrasound (size, obstruction).
- **Special tests:**
  - **Renal biopsy** — gold standard (pauci-immune crescentic GN). Do **before** high-dose steroids if possible.
  - Bronchoscopy/BAL (DAH — sequentially bloody aliquots, negative microbiology).
  - Nerve conduction studies (mononeuritis multiplex).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** 2022 ACR/EULAR classification criteria for GPA/MPA/EGPA (scoring system — see local rheumatology guidance). For bedside: any **unexplained crescentic GN + pulmonary haemorrhage** is vasculitis until proven otherwise.
- **Never-miss test:** **ANCA serology** (c-ANCA/PR3, p-ANCA/MPO). If negative but high suspicion → **renal biopsy** — omission delays life-saving treatment.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Assess for urgent dialysis** (creatinine >500, K+ >6.5, refractory pulmonary oedema, severe acidosis).
  - If DAH: **high-flow O2/CPAP**, call ITU. Avoid non-invasive ventilation if haemoptysis severe.
  - **Start steroids** after bloods sent (including cultures). Methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg IV daily ×3 days (or 1 mg/kg oral prednisolone — discuss with renal/rheum).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Induction therapy:** rituximab 375 mg/m² IV weekly ×4 (preferred in young/severe) **OR** cyclophosphamide IV (15 mg/kg every 2 weeks ×3 then every 3 weeks) or oral (2 mg/kg/day). *Check local protocol and renal function.*
  - **Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis:** co-trimoxazole 480 mg PO OD (or dapsone if allergic).
  - **GI protection:** PPI (omeprazole 20 mg OD).
  - **Bone protection:** calcium + vitamin D (colecalciferol) + bisphosphonate if prolonged steroids.
  - Monitor BP, urine output, creatinine daily.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Maintenance therapy:** rituximab 500 mg IV every 6 months (or azathioprine/mycophenolate if rituximab not tolerated).
  - Taper prednisolone slowly (over 6–12 months).
  - **Vaccinations:** influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19 (avoid live vaccines if on immunosuppression).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride IV at 50–80 mL/h if euvolaemic (avoid K+ containing).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if no bleeding or severe haemoptysis — discuss).
  - Diet: high-calorie, renal-friendly if AKI.
  - Observations: 4–6 hourly (NEWS2), UO chart, daily weight.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - **Methylprednisolone IV** 500–1000 mg daily ×3 (pulse) — then oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (max 80 mg) once daily, taper.
  - **Rituximab IV** 375 mg/m² weekly ×4 (or fixed dose 1 g ×2 given 2 weeks apart — check protocol).
  - **Cyclophosphamide IV** — dose per local protocol (e.g. 15 mg/kg every 2 weeks ×3, reduce for age >70, eGFR <20).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Oral cyclophosphamide** 2 mg/kg/day (max 200 mg) — rarely used now due to bladder toxicity.
  - **Azathioprine** 2 mg/kg PO OD (for maintenance, not induction).
  - **Mycophenolate mofetil** 500–1500 mg PO BD (if rituximab/cyclophosphamide contraindicated).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Cyclophosphamide: avoid in severe hepatic impairment; dose reduce in renal impairment; ensure adequate hydration + mesna (for haemorrhagic cystitis).
  - Rituximab: avoid in active severe infection (HBV reactivation — screen HBsAg, anti-HBc). Premedicate with paracetamol + chlorphenamine.
  - Co-trimoxazole: risk of hyperkalaemia (monitor K), especially with ACE-i.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cyclophosphamide: reduce dose by 25% if eGFR <20. Avoid if bilirubin >50.
  - Rituximab: no adjustment needed for renal/hepatic.
  - Co-trimoxazole: avoid if eGFR <15 (use dapsone).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Cyclophosphamide without mesna → haemorrhagic cystitis (potentially fatal).** Always check that mesna is prescribed for IV cyclophosphamide.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - DAH → respiratory failure, ITU admission.
  - RPGN → acute kidney injury requiring dialysis.
  - Myocarditis / pericarditis (EGPA).
  - **Infection** (especially PCP, CMV, fungal — treatable, but delay immunosuppression).
- **Chronic:**
  - End-stage renal disease (ESRD) — ~20% progress despite treatment.
  - Chronic sinus/lung damage (GPA → subglottic stenosis, saddle nose).
  - Osteoporosis (steroid-related).
  - Drug toxicity (bladder cancer with cyclophosphamide, PML with rituximab).
  - Relapse rate ~30–50% (higher in GPA).
sections.Prognosis:
- **1-year survival:** ~80–90% with prompt treatment. **Causes of death:** infection (#1), active vasculitis (DAH, renal failure), cardiovascular.
- **Chronic:** many require long-term immunosuppression. Renal recovery possible if crescentic GN caught early (<50% crescents).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always screen for infection** (chest X-ray, cultures, procalcitonin) before starting high-dose steroids — a missed infection can kill.
- **Check urine dipstick + creatinine** in every patient with unexplained haemoptysis or purpura.
- **Start PCP prophylaxis** (co-trimoxazole) on day 1 of induction — this is often forgotten.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Giving steroids before steroids?** Rule out TB and strongyloides in at-risk patients (chest X-ray, travel history). Steroids can reactivate.
- **Using NSAIDs for arthralgia** in suspected vasculitis — can worsen renal injury.
- **Delaying renal biopsy** for >48 hours after steroids start — steroids can obscure histology (pauci-immune pattern may become negative).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - **Haemoptysis, breathlessness, or dark urine** (DAH or RPGN).
  - **Fever >38°C, rigors** (infection — need urgent review and stop/withhold immunosuppression until cleared).
  - **New mononeuritis multiplex** (foot drop, wrist drop — relapse).
- **Follow-up:** Renal/rheumatology clinic within 2 weeks of discharge. Ensure ANCA titres are checked at 3–6 months (rise may predict relapse).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - Creatinine rises >50% in 24 hours or >500 μmol/L.
  - Haemoptysis >100 mL or desaturation <92%.
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2.
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours (despite fluids).
- **Call ITU / crash team if:**
  - Respiratory failure requiring NIV or intubation.
  - Shock (MAP <65) or arrhythmia with hemodynamic instability.
  - GCS <13 or seizure (possible cerebral vasculitis — rare but fatal).

rawMarkdown:
## Vasculitis — ANCA-associated

**One-liner:** Autoimmune small-vessel inflammation (GPA, MPA, EGPA) causing renal, pulmonary, ENT, skin & nerve damage — treatable but fatal if missed.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN) → hyperkalaemia / pulmonary oedema / uraemia.**  
  - Single finding: **oliguria + dipstick haematuria/proteinuria** with rising creatinine >50% in <48 hours.
- **Diffuse alveolar haemorrhage (DAH) → respiratory failure.**  
  - Single finding: **haemoptysis + bilateral airspace opacities on CXR + falling Hb** (not infection — no fever, negative procalcitonin).
- **Myocarditis (especially in EGPA) → cardiogenic shock / arrhythmia.**  
  - Single finding: **troponin rise + ECG changes (ST/T wave) without chest pain** — rule out ischaemia.

### Presentation
- **Constitutional:** fever, weight loss, fatigue, arthralgia, night sweats.
- **Renal:** haematuria (microscopic or visible), oliguria, peripheral oedema, hypertension.
- **Respiratory:** cough, dyspnoea, haemoptysis, pleuritic pain.
- **ENT (GPA):** nasal crusting, epistaxis, sinusitis, saddle-nose deformity, otitis media, subglottic stenosis.
- **Skin:** palpable purpura, ulcers, digital ischaemia, livedo racemosa.
- **Neurological:** mononeuritis multiplex (wrist drop, foot drop), cranial nerve palsy, peripheral neuropathy.
- **Other:** ocular (scleritis, episcleritis), GI (abdominal pain, bleeding), testicular pain.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Infection (endocarditis, TB, fungal)** → positive cultures/high CRP+procalcitonin; no ANCA.
- **Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture’s)** → linear IgG on renal biopsy; anti-GBM positive; ANCA negative.
- **Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)** → ANA+dsDNA; low C3/C4; photosensitive rash, serositis.
- **IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein)** → palpable purpura on lower limbs + IgA dominant immune deposits; ANCA negative.
- **Cryoglobulinaemia** → cryoglobulins + HCV serology; purpura + neuropathy.
- **Lymphoproliferative / other vasculitis** (e.g. PAN, Behçet) — ANCA negative, specific organ patterns.
- **Splitter:** **c-ANCA (PR3)** strongly suggests GPA; **p-ANCA (MPO)** suggests MPA; **p-ANCA+ MPO+ eosinophilia + asthma** → EGPA.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urinalysis (dipstick for blood, protein; microscopy for red cell casts).
  - ECG (qTc screening for cardiac involvement — especially in EGPA).
  - NEWS2 + urine output chart.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (anaemia, eosinophilia in EGPA), U+E (creatinine rapid rise), LFT, CRP/ESR (very high).
  - ANCA serology: **c-ANCA/PR3** (GPA), **p-ANCA/MPO** (MPA, EGPA). *N.B. ~10% ANCA-negative.*
  - Anti-GBM (to exclude double-positive disease), ANA, complement (C3/C4), cryoglobulins.
  - Blood cultures ×2 (rule out endocarditis).
  - Troponin + BNP if cardiac concern.
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (airspace opacities, cavities, nodules — GPA).
  - CT chest (high-res: nodules, cavitation, ground glass — DAH).
  - CT sinuses (if ENT symptoms — mucosal thickening, bony destruction).
  - Renal ultrasound (size, obstruction).
- **Special tests:**
  - **Renal biopsy** — gold standard (pauci-immune crescentic GN). Do **before** high-dose steroids if possible.
  - Bronchoscopy/BAL (DAH — sequentially bloody aliquots, negative microbiology).
  - Nerve conduction studies (mononeuritis multiplex).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** 2022 ACR/EULAR classification criteria for GPA/MPA/EGPA (scoring system — see local rheumatology guidance). For bedside: any **unexplained crescentic GN + pulmonary haemorrhage** is vasculitis until proven otherwise.
- **Never-miss test:** **ANCA serology** (c-ANCA/PR3, p-ANCA/MPO). If negative but high suspicion → **renal biopsy** — omission delays life-saving treatment.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Assess for urgent dialysis** (creatinine >500, K+ >6.5, refractory pulmonary oedema, severe acidosis).
  - If DAH: **high-flow O2/CPAP**, call ITU. Avoid non-invasive ventilation if haemoptysis severe.
  - **Start steroids** after bloods sent (including cultures). Methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg IV daily ×3 days (or 1 mg/kg oral prednisolone — discuss with renal/rheum).
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Induction therapy:** rituximab 375 mg/m² IV weekly ×4 (preferred in young/severe) **OR** cyclophosphamide IV (15 mg/kg every 2 weeks ×3 then every 3 weeks) or oral (2 mg/kg/day). *Check local protocol and renal function.*
  - **Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis:** co-trimoxazole 480 mg PO OD (or dapsone if allergic).
  - **GI protection:** PPI (omeprazole 20 mg OD).
  - **Bone protection:** calcium + vitamin D (colecalciferol) + bisphosphonate if prolonged steroids.
  - Monitor BP, urine output, creatinine daily.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Maintenance therapy:** rituximab 500 mg IV every 6 months (or azathioprine/mycophenolate if rituximab not tolerated).
  - Taper prednisolone slowly (over 6–12 months).
  - **Vaccinations:** influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19 (avoid live vaccines if on immunosuppression).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride IV at 50–80 mL/h if euvolaemic (avoid K+ containing).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if no bleeding or severe haemoptysis — discuss).
  - Diet: high-calorie, renal-friendly if AKI.
  - Observations: 4–6 hourly (NEWS2), UO chart, daily weight.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - **Methylprednisolone IV** 500–1000 mg daily ×3 (pulse) — then oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg (max 80 mg) once daily, taper.
  - **Rituximab IV** 375 mg/m² weekly ×4 (or fixed dose 1 g ×2 given 2 weeks apart — check protocol).
  - **Cyclophosphamide IV** — dose per local protocol (e.g. 15 mg/kg every 2 weeks ×3, reduce for age >70, eGFR <20).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Oral cyclophosphamide** 2 mg/kg/day (max 200 mg) — rarely used now due to bladder toxicity.
  - **Azathioprine** 2 mg/kg PO OD (for maintenance, not induction).
  - **Mycophenolate mofetil** 500–1500 mg PO BD (if rituximab/cyclophosphamide contraindicated).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Cyclophosphamide: avoid in severe hepatic impairment; dose reduce in renal impairment; ensure adequate hydration + mesna (for haemorrhagic cystitis).
  - Rituximab: avoid in active severe infection (HBV reactivation — screen HBsAg, anti-HBc). Premedicate with paracetamol + chlorphenamine.
  - Co-trimoxazole: risk of hyperkalaemia (monitor K), especially with ACE-i.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cyclophosphamide: reduce dose by 25% if eGFR <20. Avoid if bilirubin >50.
  - Rituximab: no adjustment needed for renal/hepatic.
  - Co-trimoxazole: avoid if eGFR <15 (use dapsone).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Cyclophosphamide without mesna → haemorrhagic cystitis (potentially fatal).** Always check that mesna is prescribed for IV cyclophosphamide.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - DAH → respiratory failure, ITU admission.
  - RPGN → acute kidney injury requiring dialysis.
  - Myocarditis / pericarditis (EGPA).
  - **Infection** (especially PCP, CMV, fungal — treatable, but delay immunosuppression).
- **Chronic:**
  - End-stage renal disease (ESRD) — ~20% progress despite treatment.
  - Chronic sinus/lung damage (GPA → subglottic stenosis, saddle nose).
  - Osteoporosis (steroid-related).
  - Drug toxicity (bladder cancer with cyclophosphamide, PML with rituximab).
  - Relapse rate ~30–50% (higher in GPA).

### Prognosis
- **1-year survival:** ~80–90% with prompt treatment. **Causes of death:** infection (#1), active vasculitis (DAH, renal failure), cardiovascular.
- **Chronic:** many require long-term immunosuppression. Renal recovery possible if crescentic GN caught early (<50% crescents).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always screen for infection** (chest X-ray, cultures, procalcitonin) before starting high-dose steroids — a missed infection can kill.
- **Check urine dipstick + creatinine** in every patient with unexplained haemoptysis or purpura.
- **Start PCP prophylaxis** (co-trimoxazole) on day 1 of induction — this is often forgotten.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Giving steroids before steroids?** Rule out TB and strongyloides in at-risk patients (chest X-ray, travel history). Steroids can reactivate.
- **Using NSAIDs for arthralgia** in suspected vasculitis — can worsen renal injury.
- **Delaying renal biopsy** for >48 hours after steroids start — steroids can obscure histology (pauci-immune pattern may become negative).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - **Haemoptysis, breathlessness, or dark urine** (DAH or RPGN).
  - **Fever >38°C, rigors** (infection — need urgent review and stop/withhold immunosuppression until cleared).
  - **New mononeuritis multiplex** (foot drop, wrist drop — relapse).
- **Follow-up:** Renal/rheumatology clinic within 2 weeks of discharge. Ensure ANCA titres are checked at 3–6 months (rise may predict relapse).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/consultant) if:**
  - Creatinine rises >50% in 24 hours or >500 μmol/L.
  - Haemoptysis >100 mL or desaturation <92%.
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2.
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 hours (despite fluids).
- **Call ITU / crash team if:**
  - Respiratory failure requiring NIV or intubation.
  - Shock (MAP <65) or arrhythmia with hemodynamic instability.
  - GCS <13 or seizure (possible cerebral vasculitis — rare but fatal).
